frameworks.name: ensemble representation
frameworks.one_line: population activity patterns encode sensory stimuli across output neuron channels

frameworks.name: pattern separation
frameworks.one_line: distinguishability of population responses to different stimuli over days

frameworks.name: long-term experience-dependent plasticity
frameworks.one_line: ensemble reorganization driven by passive exposure vs active learning

frameworks.name: cortical feedback circuit model
frameworks.one_line: top-down input on bulb interneurons proposed to trigger plasticity forms

core_question: How do distinct output channels of the olfactory bulb reorganize their ensemble representations across passive experience versus active discrimination learning?
key_assumptions: mitral and tufted cells form parallel output channels with potentially distinct dynamics
ensemble representations can be tracked longitudinally with chronic imaging in awake mice
pattern separation is a meaningful population-level readout of representational quality
manipulations: behavioral context: passive sensory experience vs active odor discrimination learning
output neuron layer: mitral cells vs tufted cells
time across days of chronic imaging
key_findings: both mitral and tufted populations show similar odor response profiles
passive experience reorganizes ensembles in both populations while pattern separation stays stable
active learning improves pattern separation in mitral but not tufted cells
both populations reorganize their ensemble representations during learning
interpretations: plasticity of ensemble representations is context- and output-layer dependent
cortical feedback onto bulb interneurons can trigger both representational reorganization and improved pattern separation
